item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: New object class isn't completely described. Interesting topic but the object class is a very crucial detail to the article and the SCP itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is, without a doubt, one of my favorite things on the entire site. When people ask me why I'm always reading SCP Foundation, this is one of the articles toward which I point them. I am surprised that it has such a low rating, and that so many people seem to really hate it (from the comments). Usually when I read such negative feedback, it's from earlier iterations of an article, not the current year.

I absolutely love it. The description of each item as something that's come from a (seemingly) friendly and very advanced culture that wants to learn about us from our books, but that (somehow!) doesn't understand the difference between fiction and non-fiction is ingenious.

I don't really know what to tell you to do to improve it, because it's already fantastic. From what others have said: I suppose you don't have to have all those GOIs in there, but really, where else would all these things have been if not in possession of these groups? Like I said, I think this whole thing is awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This as a whole had been one unpleasant experience to read. Had I not read Drewbear's comment on the first page, I wouldn't understand this article either. Too scattered to make sense. Obscure unifying theme.

Minus one vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This just doesn't really strike a chord with me. The narrative goes in so many different directions that I honestly got confused what you were trying to do. But my biggest problem was how ambitious it is. With all of the GoI connections and attempts to redefine them, this doesn't mesh well with my headcanon.

Now, I try to not vote based on headcanon issues. But I feel like this skip simply doesn't work well enough for me to accept it.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Contains a typo: "coated in an layer"

*fixed

item.title: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Soultear, this feedback is vague and unhelpful, as well as unnecessarily vehement. Please adhere to the site's Criticism Policy when commenting on articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's so much going on here, I hate it. It's a big pile of undefined something, set on fire.

Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really my thinking on the subject as well. On the one hand, the Zeno classification can be justified by the article being set decades from now, when the Foundation could presumably have updated/revised their classifications. On the other hand, the very fact that it is attributed to decades in the future is off-putting. Particularly since it speaks candidly and openly about future events. Even if we are not to assume the present-day Foundation is receiving information from its future incarnation - in which case specifics should absolutely be blocked out, the article requires me to buy into the author's vision of the future, and that "solenoid" will catch on as a surname in the next quarter-century…or rather, that it already has in order for "W.W. Solenoid" to be old enough to become president in the 2040's. For my tastes, that makes it too difficult to maintain suspension of disbelief.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
EDIT: Timer Removed, edits made.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

Quite a bit to work with in this one.

Each individual section of this article makes direct reference to at least of Group of Interest. All of the groups referenced are one's with guide pages, which can easily be linked to with out altering the text itself.

In the third section, reference is made to a "Fifth International" workers' union, a concept very similar to SCP-2573. However, a simple in line link to that article doesn't quite flow properly in my opinion. A small text edit allows the link to work with the flow of the text.

Change this:

SCP-2005-A was recovered from an art gallery by Her Majesty's Fellowship for the Study of Curiosities and Phantasmagoria in Marseilles in 1897, where it had been modified and installed as part of an upcoming salon des refusés exhibition, following reports of involvement from the "Nous Avons Conclu Une Entente" art collective.

To this:

SCP-2005-A was recovered from an art gallery by Her Majesty's Fellowship for the Study of Curiosities and Phantasmagoria in Marseilles in 1897, where it had been modified and installed as part of an upcoming salon des refusés exhibition, following reports of involvement from the "Nous Avons Conclu Une Entente" art collective.

Change this:

In 1945, SCP-2005-B was lost due to the defection of Area 12 to a group which was known to the Foundation as "Cách-Mạng Hàng-Cháo", or "The Chaos Insurgency".

To this:

In 1945, SCP-2005-B was lost due to the defection of Area 12 to a group which was known to the Foundation as "Cách-Mạng Hàng-Cháo", or "The Chaos Insurgency".

Change this:

Any direct connection between SCP-2005-C and what was then termed the "Northern Fifth Church" is unknown.

To this:

Any direct connection between SCP-2005-C and SCP-2573, or what was then termed the "Northern Fifth Church," is unknown.

Change this:

SCP-2005-D was initially recovered by the Prometheus Labs corporation circa 1997 for reverse-engineering (via the traditional method, as opposed to the type carried out by PL using axiom-suspension drives), and apparently fed it information as requested to pacify it.

To this:

SCP-2005-D was initially recovered by the Prometheus Labs corporation circa 1997 for reverse-engineering (via the traditional method, as opposed to the type carried out by PL using axiom-suspension drives), and apparently fed it information as requested to pacify it.

Change this:

According to declassified Pacific Storage Trust documentation, plans had been drawn for the use of SCP-2005-D's nutrient-cube technology by a wing of the Trust known as the Manna Charitable Foundation, but these were scrapped before implementation due to unspecified catastrophe.

To this:

According to declassified Pacific Storage Trust documentation, plans had been drawn for the use of SCP-2005-D's nutrient-cube technology by a wing of the Trust known as the Manna Charitable Foundation, but these were scrapped before implementation due to unspecified catastrophe.

Change this:

SCP-2005-E was recovered from the Moon in 2042, where it had made contact with a church belonging to the Reconstructionist sect of Christianity (see archived files pertaining to "The Church of the Broken God").

To this:

SCP-2005-E was recovered from the Moon in 2042, where it had made contact with a church belonging to the Reconstructionist sect of Christianity (see archived files pertaining to "The Church of the Broken God").

– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only two parts of this I don't like are the Object Class: Zeno, and the fact that the article is set in the distant future. It breaks my suspension of disbelief something fierce when I read "in 2042…".

Plus there are like four GOIs in this article, and I can't for the life of me figure out why. Especially the Manna Charitable Foundation… seriously? They're effectively the uncool hippies of the anomalous community. Why would they have an interest in alien objects?

I did enjoy parts of this article, and the concept is fantastic, but there are too many things bugging me about it to upvote. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an interesting concept, and there is a bunch of really cool stuff at the margins. However, slogging through thousands of words about probes significantly diminished my interest; by the end, I was skimming the text. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this, am sad I hadn't read it before, and don't understand its relatively low rating.

My favorite parts, I think, were the implications that the Church of the Broken God had gone mainstream and that the Chaos Insurgency's name was an incorrect translation that just… stuck. Also, that the MCF is part of the CI, because I am totes in agreement with all of that.

Actually, this whole thing perfectly fits with my headcanon in every way. Which is cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this would work really well if the different sub-skips were collapsible. Give it a clearer structure.

Loved the concept and the details, but I feel like this is one of those articles that looks much better when you look back on it with all the details finally in place, and the length makes it seem like it's just bloated unless you read to the end.

Just a theory, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Usually I read SCPs right before I go to bed (or more accurately, on the bed) so I'm not usually in my perfect consciousness when reading the article. This one was one of those SCPs that were hard to understand by my usual habits. So I took the time and thoroughly went through it like how a doctor would a latest research journal, and now I'm happy to report that I too see the elaborate construction this article hides behind.

Though I must agree that this article can be shorter, albeit with dramatic change. Perhaps the number of items can be reduced to four, and perhaps you don't really need to connect this other GoIs.. (in my opinion while mentioning other well known GoIs is an interesting installment it doesn't really do anything for SCP article itself. I'm like "OK so Chaos Insurgency is Pacific Storage Trust. Wow." "OK so the Church got their hands on this stuff first" then what?) I don't know, it's the author's decision and I'm willing to follow it.

A question I bear in mind; Why do they create probes and come to Earth to collect the data? From what I understand it almost feels like they feel the need to protect us, as they have witnessed the grim future described in bunch of fiction, so they think they are on a rescue mission. And finally by the fifth mission they are slowly realizing that the reality is vastly different from what you get from novel or movie.. Is my guess correct? Or are they just studying how 'extrasterrestrials' are and will be?

item.title: Grammar
item.content:encoded: Contained scp-2005 instances are to be contained? What if they aren't contained? How do you contain them to contain them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the concept at hand and find delight in how some of these details play out. Unfortunately, for the bulk of the article there's this voice in the back of my head constantly saying "infodump, infodump, infodump, [beat] infodump". Don't misconstrue this as "this should be a tale", because I don't like limiting what a skip can be; just, well, I feel the format does not particularly help it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why? I think it works well in the context of this article. Quoting something and then appending "and downvoted" isn't good criticism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Object Class: Zeno (Medium Containment Difficulty/Low Threat)

Aaaaaand downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm conflicted on this one. It's decent as far as SCPs go, but I've never been crazy about "future" SCPs (the few that there are). It's a bit off-putting for me, as I am reading them in what the writer would consider the "past," before it was written, so its very existence now is an anomaly. It's a personal preference thing, really. As far as tales go (and I haven't even delved into that rabbit hole yet), I think it'd bother me less. The thought on the evolution of the GOIs is interesting, though.

item.title: Re: Disgusting
item.content:encoded:
Disgusting

This file is just… horrible!

We have a Criticism Policy. There's no need for that kind of language, especially since you're new to the site. If you don't have anything polite to say, then don't say it at all.

NO information is blocked out, especially the stuff that should be such as dates, names, and such.

Redacting too much is just as obnoxious and tone-breaking as redacting too little. There's absolutely nothing wrong with that particular aspect of its presentation. Many of our best articles have no redaction or blackboxing at all.

The whole article is organized very differently and it doesn't follow and it introduces a completely new object class type.

Authors are allowed to do that, if they wish. That doesn't stop anyone from downvoting if they don't like it, of course.

It also warps information about the Chaos Insurgency.

So? We have lots of different interpretations of every single Group of Interest. We're allowed to have creative license.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Information being blacked out is not a requirement, but a literary device.

"Disgusting" - Really? Calm down there, that's not constructive criticism at all.

item.title: Re: Disgusting
item.content:encoded:
Yes, and?

Is the structure immutable? Have the dictates been writ in stone?

There has never been a requirement for blackboxing certain things, or even the object classes. They are merely traditions. If someone can succeed at writing a viable article with tradition violations, all the more power to them.

Canon is what you can get away with.

item.title: Disgusting
item.content:encoded: This file is just… horrible! NO information is blocked out, especially the stuff that should be such as dates, names, and such. The whole article is organized very differently and it doesn't follow and it introduces a completely new object class type. It also warps information about the Chaos Insurgency.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel your pain.

(actually, I don't mind new object classes)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one, especially the name, a good name can really draw people in.

item.title: Zeno class?
item.content:encoded: Seem's like everybody's trying out new object classes with the contest….which is not a bad thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i thought it was clever, because their Achilles isn't catching up to our tortoise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's what I came to ask.
It takes people to where it thinks they will be safe, I get that. But how did it know where to put the first one? Just a random drop?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2000-C: There is a traveler from beyond the stars: gleaming, hopeful. He understands your experiences with such spacemen have caused much suffering; he pleads welcome, disarmed, and approaches the local library.

And the "Starman" chorus started to play in my head. Nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, I've better addressed the concerns that it dragged on and had tacked-on GOI references.

In order to help deal with these issues, I have trimmed some superfluous detail in favor of more engaging and explanatory information, and I have made the GOI references a larger part of the article, so that they remain consistent throughout instead of sporadic and half-involved.

In other words, the article is now longer and has more GOI content. That should fix it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but it drags out and a lot of the GOI references feel really shoehorned in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You aren't the first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Object class is Zeno?

I've never heard of that classification before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The cutting does improve its readability.

I think it's unclear - to the reader who doesn't know - what exactly the concept actually is. I honestly think you should straight up TELL the reader with an additional second paragraph.

I also think organization of detail can use some work — -E and -D have perhaps excessive detail (such as pointlessly specific measurements) and the paragraphing is odd (you could use more paragraphing in general).

Also I think you lose something by removing the recovery dates for most of them. Possibly quite a bit.

I think it would be better to clearly separate each section, such as a line break (possibly with an actual line), then "SCP-2000-[X] — Recovered X/X/XXXX" and then another line break. Or some similar organizational method.

(I still like it, btw.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nous Avons Conclu Une Entente

You madman.

Actually, I love how this tells part of the story of the Foundation and its interactions with various GOIs through time. The various pieces are pretty marvelous too: the awful barber blimp that pretends to clean while being watched; the robot that talks in Markov chains of HG Wells fiction; the fucking video game HUD device. This is a lot of really interesting stuff, and the narrative thread is subtle but absolutely there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can guess what that early redaction in the document is covering.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I tried to shorten it, but I couldn't tell what to cut, and couldn't get anyone to play unbiased editor and advise me. I tried just cutting everything extraneous and got rid of over 1100 words, but the people who actually liked it said "what no, you removed everything interesting". So I put roughly 80% of the lost material back while generally tightening it up, even if it's still way too much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do you not understand yourselves?

Please clarify.

This is perfect. I finally read the skip, and I still think that it should be three-four times shorter than it is now, but this is a perfect ending for what you're doing, and I can't not upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These 25-gram green-tinted blocks contain a mixture of synthetic proteins, fats, and carbohydrates that is suitable for long-term human consumption. A test subject reported that nutrient cubes "taste like mint and salad dressing".

… so, we should make sure those blocks never touch a dead body?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh. In its current form, the references to AWCY (?) and the Fifthists feel randomly tacked on.

SCP-2000-D will then bring the human to the most recent safe location where a human was deposited

I don't fully understand this sentence. Where, exactly, is this "safe location"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know you've edited down for complexity, but this is still not flowing correctly from my viewpoint. It's weird, because I know there's a unifying theme to what's going on here and I know what you're attempting, but even with that it's just not reading like a coherent narrative to me.

The individual components to what is going on are written well enough, and there's enough mystery left to be intriguing, but it's still coming across as muddled. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've trimmed/removed several sections (like the Chao's Insurgency bit) and will be continuing to revise this SCP for length and complexity.

Edit: Okay, all the GOI stuff and references to other SCPs are gone now as well, and some details about drone behavior have been cut.

Edit 2: And now the documents are all current except for the 1940 one.

Edit 3: Still trimmed for brevity and relative simplicity, but 90% of the references are back in, because I like worldbuilding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Soooooooo… are those indeed veiled 447 references, or am I reading too much into it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Nous Avons Conclu Une Entente"

Heh.

There's so much going on here! I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd really rather that you unexpunge the star they're originating from, since there are only 2 that are approximately the right distance away (Teegarden's star or SCR 1845-6357)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's aliens sending probes/assist devices based on science fiction they acquire, apparently thinking that it's a serious factual prediction. But because of light-speed limitations, the things they send arrive about 25 years after the scifi they were based on was written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
however, the accuracy of these reports is heavily disputed due to the nature of certain anomalies under the control of the Afro-Pacific Storage Trust.]//

Formatting issue?

Overall there is a whole lot of stuff here and it's very difficult to get through comprehensively in my opinion. There's a lot of headcanon here, there's a lot of hints, there's a lot of everything and it feels like it becomes mucky.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sorry, I have absolutely no buttfucking clue what any of this is all about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be fair, I haven't read it thoroughly yet.

But creating a new GOI is the first thing you need to do. Utterly required. Go. Doesn't even have to be related to your entry. Do it. Right now. No excuses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, they're all effectively the same anomaly, in terms of what they can do and where they came from. It's functionally little different from, say, different 609 instances.

Besides, the last thing I need to do is create another GOI.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Any damn fool can predict the past." — Larry Niven.

+1

E: Also, the title of this reads like the name of a Hanna Barbera cartoon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It just occurred to me: would the Foundation classify all of these as the same SCP? I know they need to be for the contest, but realistically it seems like they would be different articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I quite loved this, again as I said elsewhere. I like the detail work, and I like the future elements, and I really like the concept and theme. Nicely done.
